title: Lablabi
subtitle: Tunisian chickpea soup with bread and egg
excerpt: Chickpeas simmered in a cumin-and-garlic broth, ladled over torn stale bread in a bowl, then finished with a poached egg, olive oil, harissa, capers, and lemon. The bread soaks up the broth and swells. The egg breaks into the soup. Tunisian street food eaten for breakfast at roadside stalls.
sourceNotes: Lablabi is the Tunisian breakfast soup, sold from dedicated stalls where customers line up at dawn. The dish is assembled to order: stale bread torn into the bowl, chickpea broth ladled over, condiments added from the table. Each customer seasons their own bowl. It is one of the most distinctively Tunisian dishes.

recipe.batchNotes: The chickpea broth keeps refrigerated for 3 days. Assemble fresh each time.

content.text: Lablabi is an assembly dish. The chickpea broth is simple and quick to make. The magic is in the layering of condiments into the bowl: harissa first, then olive oil, lemon, capers, and finally the egg. Each element stays distinct until you stir it in yourself at the table.

Stale bread is not optional — it must be dry enough to absorb the broth rather than disintegrating immediately. A day-old baguette or sourdough torn into rough 3 cm pieces is ideal. The bread fills the bowl first, the broth is ladled over the top, and the bread swells and softens as it absorbs the liquid.

Heat the {{olive-oil}} in a large pot over medium heat. Add the {{garlic}} and {{cumin-ground}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the drained {{chickpeas}} and 1.2 litres of water. Bring to a simmer and cook for 15 minutes. Taste and add {{sea-salt-fine}}. The broth should be flavoursome — adjust cumin and salt as needed. To assemble: tear day-old bread into rough pieces and place in the bottom of each bowl. Ladle the hot chickpea broth over the bread. Add a teaspoon of {{harissa}} to each bowl. Pour 1 tablespoon of good {{olive-oil}} into each bowl. Add a few {{capers}} and a squeeze of {{lemon-juice}}. If using, lower a poached {{eggs}} on top. Serve immediately with extra harissa and lemon on the side.

items.symptom: Bread completely dissolved into the broth
items.cause: Bread too fresh or too fine-crumbed
items.fix: Use day-old or older bread — it should be stale enough to crumble slightly when torn. Dense bread like sourdough or country loaf holds up better than soft white bread

items.cause: Tinned chickpeas not cooked down long enough
items.fix: Simmer for 20 minutes rather than 15. Mash a quarter of the chickpeas against the side of the pot to release starch and thicken the broth

items.cause: Not enough harissa or lemon in the bowl
items.fix: Lablabi is meant to be seasoned aggressively at the table. The harissa and lemon are structural components, not optional extras — use them generously

content.text: Lablabi is a social institution in Tunisia. Dedicated stalls open before dawn and serve workers, students, and the hungry all morning. The bowl is deep, the bread abundant, and the condiment table crowded with harissa, olive oil, lemon, capers, tinned tuna, and chopped vegetables. Each person customises their bowl. In Tunisian diaspora communities in France, lablabi remains a comfort food, recreated at home. Outside Tunisia it is almost unknown — which makes it one of the most surprising introductions a Tunisian meal can offer.
